summary: Gruber covers Apple's surprise March 2020 product announcements: new iPad Pros, a Magic Keyboard with trackpad, system-wide pointer support in iPadOS 13.4, and a significantly improved MacBook Air. He's most excited about iPadOS pointer support, which he considers far more thoughtful than simply porting Mac-style cursors to the iPad. The new Magic Keyboard transforms the iPad Pro into a genuine laptop competitor, and Gruber argues iPads genuinely needed trackpad support for ergonomics and precision. The new MacBook Air hits the psychologically important $999 price point with doubled storage, better performance, and the scissor-switch keyboard — all with no tradeoffs.
key_points: iPadOS 13.4's pointer support is context-aware and deeply considered — a circular cursor, morphing hover states, and size-adaptive I-beam cursors rather than a simple port of Mac cursors

The Magic Keyboard transforms iPad Pro into a true clamshell laptop with floating hinge, real keys, trackpad, and no kickstand — Apple's first serious attempt at using an iOS device primarily as a laptop
Gruber draws a sharp distinction: iPads needed trackpad support for ergonomics and precision, while touchscreen Macs are at best a nice-to-have that only sounds good
The new MacBook Air hits $999 with 256GB storage, scissor-switch keyboard, and better performance — saving $300 over the previous equivalent with zero tradeoffs
New iPad Pros include a LiDAR sensor for AR, though Gruber notes the curious omission of Portrait mode on the rear camera despite hardware that should support it
money_quotes: This mouse pointer support is rich and deep — it is far more than a simplistic virtual finger tip, and far more thoughtful and graceful and direct than a port to iOS of Mac-style mouse cursors.
Touchscreen support for Macs is, at best, a nice-to-have idea. (And if you ask me, it's a feature that only sounds good.)
With this new Magic Keyboard, if it works as well as it looks like it does, Apple quite possibly has jumped to the head of the pack.
It turns it into a matter of OS preference, not form factor.
Magic is better than smart, at least when it comes to iPad keyboard covers.
It's the first attempt from Apple at a way to use an iOS device primarily as a laptop.
Today's new MacBook Air models are terrific in two regards: they're noticeably better computers at noticeably lower prices.
You save $300, get a much better keyboard, faster performance, and there is no tradeoff. It's just better and costs less.
key_insight: Apple's new iPadOS pointer support and Magic Keyboard represent the company's first serious attempt to make the iPad a genuine laptop alternative, distinguished not by compromised hardware but by OS preference.
